runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage
runs.reasoningSummary: Recollection of EITC expansions leading to sizable employment gains for US single mothers implies moderate-to-high participation response to net wage changes; literature often clusters around 0.4-0.7 but medium-run effects can be larger due to persistence and entry costs, with wide uncertainty.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage","point_estimate":0.5,"quantiles":{"p05":0.15,"p25":0.3,"p50":0.5,"p75":0.8,"p95":1.4},"citations":["Eissa and Liebman (1996)","Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001)"],"reasoning_summary":"Recollection of EITC expansions leading to sizable employment gains for US single mothers implies moderate-to-high participation response to net wage changes; literature often clusters around 0.4-0.7 but medium-run effects can be larger due to persistence and entry costs, with wide uncertainty."}

- Definition: Percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage.
- Target interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage
- Population/context: Single mothers in the United States
